description: Granular skill taxonomy for IELTS Speaking, derived from official IELTS Speaking Band Descriptors across the four marking criteria.

categories.description: Ability to speak at length without unnatural hesitation and with logical organisation

skills.description: Speaking at a natural pace without excessive hesitation, repetition or self-correction

ranks.1: Frequent long pauses, heavy reliance on fillers (um, uh), cannot sustain speech for more than a few words.
ranks.2: Noticeable hesitation and repetition; manages short turns but loses fluency on complex topics.
ranks.3: Mostly fluent with occasional hesitation; self-correction does not significantly impede communication.
ranks.4: Speaks fluently with only occasional hesitation; maintains flow even on abstract topics.
ranks.5: Near-native fluency — natural pace, minimal hesitation, smooth self-correction when needed.

skills.description: Organising ideas logically and connecting them clearly when speaking at length

ranks.1: Ideas are disorganised and hard to follow; no clear beginning, middle or end to responses.
ranks.2: Basic logical sequence in simple responses; loses coherence in extended or complex answers.
ranks.3: Mostly coherent responses; uses discourse markers to connect ideas in Part 2 and Part 3.
ranks.4: Consistently coherent with clear development; naturally uses a range of linking expressions.
ranks.5: Highly coherent responses with sophisticated organisation and seamless idea development.

skills.description: Developing answers beyond one or two sentences with relevant detail and reasoning

ranks.1: Gives one-word or very short answers; cannot extend beyond the minimum response.
ranks.2: Extends simple answers but cannot sustain a long turn in Part 2 or develop abstract ideas.
ranks.3: Extends most answers adequately; Part 2 responses reach the required length.
ranks.4: Consistently develops extended answers with examples, reasons and explanations.
ranks.5: Expertly extended answers — naturally develops and elaborates on all topics at length.

categories.description: Range, precision and naturalness of vocabulary used in speech

skills.description: Using a variety of words and expressions rather than repeating the same limited vocabulary

ranks.1: Very limited vocabulary; frequent repetition of the same basic words.
ranks.2: Uses familiar vocabulary but over-relies on a small set of words for all topics.
ranks.3: Adequate range for most familiar topics; some repetition on abstract or specialist subjects.
ranks.4: Good range across all topics; uses less common and idiomatic expressions appropriately.
ranks.5: Wide, flexible vocabulary — uses precise, idiomatic and topic-specific language naturally.

skills.description: Ability to express meaning using alternative words when the exact word is unavailable

ranks.1: Stops completely when a word is unavailable; cannot find alternative expressions.
ranks.2: Attempts paraphrase but the alternative is often vague or unclear.
ranks.3: Uses paraphrase and circumlocution adequately in most situations.
ranks.4: Confidently paraphrases with clear, accurate alternatives; rarely impeded by vocabulary gaps.
ranks.5: Expert circumlocution — seamlessly describes concepts using multiple alternative expressions.

categories.description: Variety and correctness of grammatical structures used in speech

skills.description: Using a variety of simple and complex grammatical structures in speech

ranks.1: Only simple sentences; no attempt at complex structures.
ranks.2: Attempts complex sentences but with limited variety; mostly relies on simple structures.
ranks.3: Mix of simple and complex structures; uses conditionals, relative clauses and passives sometimes.
ranks.4: Good range of complex structures; uses a variety of tenses and clause types naturally.
ranks.5: Wide grammatical range — flexibly uses all complex structures including hypotheticals and modals.

skills.description: Producing grammatically correct speech with minimal errors that impede communication

ranks.1: Frequent errors in basic structures that impede understanding.
ranks.2: Errors in complex structures but basic communication is maintained.
ranks.3: Mostly accurate; errors in complex structures do not impede communication.
ranks.4: High accuracy across simple and complex structures; rare errors that do not confuse.
ranks.5: Near-perfect accuracy — errors are rare and do not affect communication at all.

categories.description: Clarity, intelligibility and natural features of spoken English

skills.description: Being clearly understood by the listener without excessive effort

ranks.1: Very difficult to understand; listener must ask for frequent repetition.
ranks.2: Intelligible for familiar topics but difficult to follow on complex content.
ranks.3: Mostly intelligible; occasional unclear words do not impede overall understanding.
ranks.4: Consistently intelligible; accent does not impede communication.
ranks.5: Fully intelligible at all times; clear and natural pronunciation throughout.

skills.description: Using stress, rhythm and intonation naturally to convey meaning

ranks.1: Flat delivery with no sentence stress or intonation variation; sounds robotic.
ranks.2: Some word stress but sentence-level stress and intonation are mostly absent.
ranks.3: Uses stress and intonation in most sentences; some unnatural patterns remain.
ranks.4: Natural stress and intonation in most contexts; enhances rather than impedes meaning.
ranks.5: Native-like prosody — stress, rhythm and intonation are natural and communicatively effective.
